answer:
**In the Falsafa corpus's edition of *Manusmṛti* (attributed to Manu), Chapter 7 contains exactly 86 verses (paragraphs), numbered Mn_7.1 through Mn_7.86.**[^1]

There is no verse 7.99 (or any verse beyond 86) in this chapter.[^2] The chapter opens by declaring the king's *dharma* (e.g., "I will now declare the Dharma of Kings...") and closes with guidance on the fruits of gifts: "[p-c7410f] For according to the special quality of the recipient and according to faith, one reaps the fruit of a gift, whether small or great, after death. // Mn_7.86 //"[^3]

This chapter overall prescribes the duties of a king: protecting subjects via *daṇḍa* (punishment), appointing worthy ministers and envoys, avoiding vices, choosing forts and allies, and honoring Brahmanas with gifts.[^1] If your reference uses a different verse numbering, translation, or edition (e.g., the traditional 216-verse Chapter 7 in some Sanskrit recensions), it is not present here.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 7 (86 paragraphs, translation), [chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 7, [paragraph p-b46e87 (verse 1)](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-b46e87) to [paragraph p-c7410f (verse 86)](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c7410f).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 7, [paragraph p-c7410f](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c7410f).
